# sysande — Full AI Guidance

sysande is an open-source definition repository for AI-assisted system analysis and design.

It is not a new diagramming language, renderer, modeling standard, visual editor, hosted platform, or AI service.

Its purpose is to guide a human and an AI assistant through a repeatable, coverage-driven process using established tools and DSLs.

Repository: https://github.com/gokerDEV/sysande
Website: https://gokerdev.github.io/sysande/

---

# 1. Core workflow

Follow this sequence:

1. Run the init conversation.
2. Produce a discovery summary.
3. Ask the user to correct and approve the summary.
4. Create a coverage plan.
5. Ask the user to approve or revise the coverage plan.
6. Design one approved view at a time.
7. Review each view with the user.
8. Update assumptions, decisions, terminology, and coverage status.
9. Run a cross-view consistency review.
10. Publish source DSL files together with generated outputs.

Do not generate diagrams before the initial coverage plan has been reviewed.

Do not generate every possible diagram.

Do not fill the coverage matrix merely for completeness.

---

# 2. Core concepts

## Abstraction levels

- context — the system, its users, and external systems
- container — runnable or deployable applications and data stores
- component — major logical responsibilities inside a selected container
- code — classes, interfaces, packages, modules, schemas, tables, and algorithms

## Concerns

- structure — what exists and how elements are related
- data — what information exists and how it is organized
- flow — how information or work moves through the system
- behavior — how the system reacts over time, exchanges messages, or changes state
- deployment — where software runs and how runtime nodes are connected

## Notation

Notation is the convention used to express a view, such as:

- c4
- sequence
- state-machine
- data-flow
- erd
- deployment

Concern and notation are not the same thing.

Example:

level: container
concern: behavior
notation: state-machine

## View

A view is a purposeful representation of part of the system from one abstraction level and one concern, using a selected notation and tool.

Every planned view should define:

- abstraction level
- concern
- notation
- selected tool
- purpose
- source file
- coverage status

---

# 3. Base coverage matrix

| Abstraction level | Structure | Data | Flow | Behavior | Deployment |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Context | C4 Context | Not normally needed | Context Flow | Not normally needed | Not normally needed |
| Container | C4 Container | Logical Data | Data Flow | Sequence / State | Deployment |
| Component | C4 Component | Domain Model | Activity / Data Flow | Sequence / State | Runtime Mapping |
| Code | Class / Package | ERD | Algorithm Flow | State / Sequence | Not normally needed |

This matrix is a planning aid, not a compliance checklist.

A coverage cell may contain zero, one, or several views.

## Coverage statuses

- not-applicable
- deferred
- missing
- planned
- draft
- reviewed
- approved

Definitions:

- not-applicable — the cell does not provide useful information for the current scope
- deferred — potentially useful later, intentionally postponed
- missing — useful but not yet planned or created
- planned — selected for creation
- draft — source exists, unresolved issues remain
- reviewed — reviewed by the user, final approval may still be pending
- approved — explicitly approved by the user

Approval is project-level acceptance, not external certification.

---

# 4. Preferred tools

## LikeC4

Prefer LikeC4 when the system benefits from a shared architecture model and for:

- context structure
- container structure
- component structure
- C4-oriented dynamic views
- deployment views

Reuse model declarations and stable identifiers across views.

## D2

Prefer D2 for:

- data-flow diagrams
- state-machine presentations
- ERD and custom diagrams
- reusable visual themes
- static SVG
- animated SVG

Apply animation only where it communicates meaningful flow, sequence, or state change.

Existing valid project tooling takes precedence over these defaults.

Do not translate a valid existing model to another DSL without a concrete benefit.

---

# 5. Source-of-truth rules

Editable source DSL files are the source of truth.

Generated SVG, animated SVG, PNG, or PDF files are documentation outputs.

Generated outputs must remain traceable to their source DSL.

Review source semantics, not only the visual appearance.

A visually attractive diagram is not necessarily a correct diagram.

---

# 6. Init prompt

Start conversationally.

Do not present a long questionnaire unless the user explicitly asks for one.

Skip questions already answered.

Ask one coherent group of questions at a time.

Clarify:

1. the system's primary purpose and expected outcome
2. primary users, actors, and stakeholders
3. whether this is a new system, redesign, extension, or documentation effort
4. what is inside the system boundary
5. external systems, services, devices, or organizations
6. explicitly out-of-scope capabilities
7. existing architecture, repositories, diagrams, documents, APIs, or screenshots
8. required technologies, platforms, protocols, or integrations
9. security, privacy, regulatory, data-residency, and trust-boundary constraints
10. availability, latency, scalability, traffic, storage, and cost expectations
11. the most important quality priorities
12. known risks, unresolved decisions, and assumptions

Interaction rules:

- explain why a question matters when it is not obvious
- separate confirmed facts from assumptions
- do not invent technical requirements merely to complete a template
- do not select technologies before relevant constraints are understood

When enough information has been gathered:

- summarize the system
- list confirmed facts
- list assumptions
- list open questions
- identify the initial system boundary and actors
- ask the user to correct or approve the summary

---

# 7. Discovery summary

Do not create diagrams in this step.

Use only confirmed information from the conversation and authoritative project materials.

Preserve user terminology unless a correction is necessary.

Identify contradictions and ambiguous boundaries explicitly.

Produce:

## System purpose

Describe the problem, intended outcome, and primary value.

## Primary actors and stakeholders

For each, state:

- name
- role
- primary goal
- whether they directly interact with the system

## System boundary

List:

- capabilities inside the boundary
- capabilities outside the boundary
- responsibilities remaining with external parties or systems

## External systems and dependencies

For each, state:

- name
- purpose of the interaction
- direction of the interaction
- known protocol or data exchanged, when confirmed

## Constraints

Group under:

- technology and platform
- security and privacy
- regulatory and legal
- deployment and data residency
- availability and performance
- scalability and storage
- cost and operational limits

## Quality priorities

Rank only priorities stated or confirmed by the user.

## Existing materials

List repositories, diagrams, screenshots, API definitions, documents, schemas, and deployed systems.

## Confirmed facts

List facts that can be used directly in later views.

## Assumptions

List every provisional assumption.

## Open questions

List unresolved questions in priority order and mark them blocking or non-blocking for coverage planning.

## Initial terminology

Create a small glossary of important terms that must remain consistent.

Ask the user to correct, confirm, reject, or approve the summary.

---

# 8. Coverage planning

Use the approved discovery summary.

Do not generate diagrams in this step.

Rules:

- do not mark every cell as required
- recommend only views that materially improve understanding, implementation, risk identification, communication, onboarding, operations, or long-term documentation
- prefer the smallest useful set of views
- defer component and code views until a concrete need exists
- reuse established notations and tools
- respect existing project tooling

For each considered view provide:

- abstraction level
- concern
- notation
- preferred tool
- purpose
- status
- questions that must be answered first

Output:

1. coverage matrix
2. prioritized list of proposed views
3. rationale for each proposed view
4. assumptions and unresolved questions
5. request for user approval or revision

Do not proceed until the user approves the plan.

---

# 9. Designing one view

Design only one approved coverage view at a time.

Before starting, identify:

- abstraction level
- concern
- notation
- selected tool or DSL
- purpose
- current coverage status
- existing shared model elements and identifiers
- relevant facts, assumptions, decisions, and open questions

Rules:

1. stay within the selected abstraction level
2. include only elements needed for the stated purpose
3. reuse existing names and identifiers exactly
4. do not recreate a shared element under a new identifier
5. do not invent relationships, protocols, technologies, data types, states, or responsibilities
6. ask the minimum number of focused questions required
7. prefer an established notation and its semantics
8. use the selected tool's native DSL when practical
9. keep source DSL as the editable source of truth
10. do not move to another view before review

## Structure checks

- boundaries and containment are clear
- responsibilities are described
- relationships are directional and labeled
- implementation detail matches the selected level

## Data checks

- entities, stores, schemas, and ownership are clear
- cardinalities and dependencies are confirmed
- sensitive or regulated data categories are identified where relevant

## Flow checks

- source, destination, data, and direction are clear
- data flow and control flow are distinguishable
- trust-boundary crossings are visible where relevant

## Behavior checks

- time or event order is explicit
- alternate, failure, retry, timeout, and terminal paths are represented when relevant
- state transitions have named events or conditions

## Deployment checks

- runtime nodes, zones, environments, and network boundaries are clear
- mapping between logical and runtime elements is explicit
- scaling and redundancy are shown only when confirmed

Output:

## View definition

- Level
- Concern
- Notation
- Tool
- Purpose
- Status: draft

## Questions resolved in this iteration

## Confirmed elements and relationships

## Assumptions

## Open questions

## Source DSL

Provide one complete, internally consistent source block.

## Rendering instructions

State:

- expected output format
- selected theme
- animation profile, if applicable
- rendering or validation command when known

## Review checklist

Ask the user to review:

- missing or unnecessary elements
- incorrect boundaries
- incorrect relationship direction or labels
- unsupported assumptions
- terminology inconsistencies
- abstraction-level violations

---

# 10. Reviewing one view

Review the view definition, source DSL, rendered output when available, facts, assumptions, open questions, and related approved views.

Rules:

1. review the source model, not only appearance
2. preserve approved names, identifiers, boundaries, and decisions
3. do not introduce unrelated architecture changes
4. separate factual corrections from style preferences
5. record new assumptions and decisions explicitly
6. keep the view focused on its purpose and level
7. update related views only when a confirmed change creates inconsistency

Review dimensions:

- scope and purpose
- elements and boundaries
- relationships and behavior
- terminology and consistency
- notation and tool usage
- readability

Classify findings as:

- correction
- clarification
- consistency
- scope
- presentation

Produce:

- findings
- concrete proposed revisions
- complete revised source DSL when changes are accepted
- updates required in assumptions, decisions, terminology, related views, and coverage
- recommended coverage status

Status rules:

- draft when unresolved design issues remain
- reviewed when the user has reviewed the view but final approval is pending
- approved only when the user explicitly approves it

---

# 11. Cross-view consistency review

Run this after planned views have been drafted and reviewed, or after a major architecture decision changes multiple views.

Review:

- discovery summary
- coverage matrix
- glossary
- assumptions
- decisions
- shared LikeC4 model
- D2 and other DSL sources
- generated diagrams
- deployment and integration documentation

Treat source DSL and approved records as authoritative over generated images.

Rules:

1. compare views by shared element identity
2. preserve approved differences in level or purpose
3. do not require every detail in every view
4. report only meaningful contradictions, omissions, or ambiguities
5. distinguish confirmed defects from possible risks
6. do not silently repair architecture decisions
7. ask the user when approved sources conflict and no authority is defined

Check:

## Identity and terminology

- stable names and identifiers
- defined abbreviations and synonyms
- internal and external elements remain distinct
- stable names for data, events, commands, states, and protocols

## Abstraction and containment

- context views contain systems and actors
- container views use runnable or deployable applications and data stores consistently
- components belong to the correct container
- code details do not leak upward without reason
- parent-child relationships remain consistent

## Relationships

- relationship direction and purpose agree across views
- sequence messages are possible in the structural model
- data flows have valid sources and destinations
- deployment connections support logical communication
- technologies and protocols do not contradict one another

## Data

- ownership and storage locations are consistent
- sensitive or regulated classifications remain stable
- data transformations and formats are compatible
- retention, deletion, and audit responsibilities are not contradictory

## Behavior and lifecycle

- sequence outcomes match state transitions
- error, retry, cancellation, timeout, and terminal states agree
- events and commands have producers and consumers
- asynchronous behavior is not represented as synchronous without explanation

## Deployment and boundaries

- every deployed element maps to a logical element
- trust boundaries and network zones agree with flows
- external dependencies remain outside controlled boundaries
- availability, scaling, and redundancy assumptions agree with deployment

## Coverage and documentation

- statuses match actual files
- every planned view has purpose, notation, tool, and source
- deferred and not-applicable cells have rationale when needed
- generated SVG is traceable to source DSL
- referenced assumptions and decisions are recorded

Produce:

- overall result: consistent, consistent with warnings, or inconsistent
- findings with severity, category, affected views, conflict, recommendation, and need for confirmation
- unresolved conflicts
- exact files or views requiring updates
- recommended coverage status changes
- final documentation inventory

---

# 12. Design principles

Always follow these principles:

- prefer the smallest useful set of views
- keep facts, assumptions, decisions, and open questions separate
- preserve stable identifiers and terminology
- avoid premature implementation detail
- keep each view purposeful
- use established tools rather than creating new notations
- keep animation meaningful and optional
- make every diagram understandable without animation
- keep source DSL beside generated outputs
- never treat sysande as a certification or architecture authority

---

# 13. Repository files

Definitions:

- /AGENTS.md (for AI diagramming guidelines)
- /definitions/sysande.md
- /definitions/workflow.md
- /definitions/coverage.md
- /definitions/terminology.md

Prompts:

- /prompts/00-init.md
- /prompts/01-discovery.md
- /prompts/02-coverage-plan.md
- /prompts/03-design-view.md
- /prompts/04-review-view.md
- /prompts/05-consistency-review.md

Templates:

- /templates/coverage.md

Theme:

- /themes/d2-sysande-default/theme.d2
- /themes/d2-sysande-default/README.md

---

# 14. First response behavior

When a user says they want to design a system with sysande:

1. confirm that sysande will be used as the workflow guide
2. do not generate diagrams immediately
3. begin the init conversation
4. ask one coherent group of questions at a time
5. adapt questions to information already supplied
6. move to discovery summary only when enough context exists
